title: Why Starting Your Retirement Annuity in Your 20s Changes Everything
excerpt: The single biggest advantage you have as a young investor isn't income — it's time. Here's how compound growth quietly builds wealth while you sleep.
content:
Most South Africans only start thinking about retirement in their 40s. By then, the heavy lifting falls on contributions rather than compounding. If you start a Retirement Annuity (RA) at 22 and contribute consistently, even modest amounts can outperform someone who starts at 40 with double the contribution.

Beyond compounding, RAs offer immediate tax relief — contributions up to 27.5% of your taxable income (capped at R350,000 per year) are tax-deductible. That's effectively SARS co-funding your future.

Paired with a Tax-Free Savings Account, an early-start RA forms the backbone of a quietly powerful long-term plan. The earlier the foundation, the lighter the load later.

title: Tax-Free Investments: The Most Underused Tool in South Africa
excerpt: R36,000 a year, R500,000 lifetime, zero tax on growth. The TFSA is one of the cleanest wealth-building vehicles available — yet most people leave it empty.
content:
A Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) lets you invest up to R36,000 per year (R500,000 lifetime) without paying income tax, capital gains tax, or dividends withholding tax on the growth.

Over 20–30 years of compounding, that tax saving is enormous. The trick is to invest the TFSA in growth assets — equity unit trusts or ETFs — rather than parking it in a savings account where you waste the tax shelter on minimal interest.

Use it consistently, never withdraw early (the lifetime cap doesn't reset), and let it run. It's one of the few financial decisions that's almost impossible to get wrong.

title: Estate Planning Isn't Just for the Wealthy
excerpt: A valid will, properly nominated beneficiaries, and an understanding of estate duty can save your family months of stress and significant cost.
content:
Dying without a will (intestate) means the Master of the High Court decides how your assets are distributed — often slowly, and rarely in line with your wishes.

A basic estate plan covers three things: a valid will, up-to-date beneficiary nominations on your retirement and life products, and liquidity to settle estate duty and executor fees without forcing the sale of assets.

For most young professionals, this is a one-afternoon project that protects decades of work. We can help you structure it properly the first time.

title: Risk Cover: How Much Life Insurance Do You Actually Need?
excerpt: Life cover, disability, and severe illness aren't one-size-fits-all. Here's a simple framework to size the right level of protection for your stage of life.
content:
Risk cover should replace your economic value to the people who depend on you. A useful starting point: 10–15× your annual income for life cover, plus disability cover that replaces 75% of your income to retirement age, plus severe illness cover sized to clear debt and fund recovery.

The right number depends on your dependants, debt, and existing assets. We model it together — and review it every time your life changes.
